county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Louisiana parish formed 1843; antebellum parish; North Louisiana uplands
county_metadata.research_tips: Louisiana State Archives and Franklin Parish Clerk of Court (Winnsboro). Formed 1843 from Catahoula, Ouachita, and Madison. Northeast Louisiana delta cotton country. Pre-1843 records split between parent parishes. Winnsboro area families. Anglo-American settlement predominates. Protestant church records (Baptist, Methodist) are important sources. Franklin Parish Library has local genealogical materials. Check Catahoula (burned) for earlier records — Louisiana State Archives may have surviving copies. Cross-reference with Richland and Madison parishes for related families. Boeuf River area plantation records.

county_metadata.record_losses: 1878 courthouse fire destroyed early records; Colfax Massacre (1873) disrupted record keeping
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Louisiana parish formed 1869; Reconstruction-era formation; Central Louisiana
county_metadata.research_tips: Louisiana State Archives and Grant Parish Clerk of Court (Colfax). Formed 1869 from Rapides and Winn. BURNED PARISH — 1878 courthouse fire destroyed early records. Site of the Colfax Massacre (1873) — one of the deadliest incidents of racial violence in Reconstruction. Pre-1869 records in Rapides and Winn. Check Louisiana State Archives for surviving state copies. Central Louisiana piney woods region. Protestant church records supplement lost civil records. Grant Parish Library has limited local history. Mixed Anglo-American and Creole heritage. Timber and farming economy. Pollock and Georgetown area families.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Louisiana parish formed 1868; Reconstruction-era formation; Acadian/Cajun heartland; significant free people of color community
county_metadata.research_tips: Louisiana State Archives and Iberia Parish Clerk of Court (New Iberia). Formed 1868 from St. Martin and St. Mary. Named for the Iberian Peninsula — reflecting Spanish heritage. New Iberia is a center of Cajun and Creole culture. Shadows-on-the-Teche plantation (National Trust) has extensive archives. Avery Island (Tabasco sauce, salt dome) area. Catholic church registers at St. Peter's Church (New Iberia). Sugar plantation records. Significant free people of color community in the antebellum period. Iberia Parish Library has local history. Acadians, Spanish (Malaguenos), and French Creoles all have heritage here.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Louisiana parish formed 1807; original Orleans Territory parish; River Road plantation region; significant free people of color community
county_metadata.research_tips: Louisiana State Archives and Iberville Parish Clerk of Court (Plaquemine). Original Orleans Territory parish (1807). Named for Pierre Le Moyne d'Iberville, French explorer. Major sugar plantation parish along the Mississippi River. Extensive enslaved persons records in notarial archives and succession files. Catholic church registers at Immaculate Conception (Plaquemine) date to colonial era. French and Spanish colonial records. Nottoway Plantation area. Iberville Parish Library has local history. Cross-reference with Ascension and West Baton Rouge for related families. Bayou Plaquemine and River Road families.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Louisiana parish formed 1845; antebellum parish; North Louisiana uplands
county_metadata.research_tips: Louisiana State Archives and Jackson Parish Clerk of Court (Jonesboro). Formed 1845 from Claiborne, Ouachita, and Union. North Louisiana uplands — piney woods and cotton farming. Pre-1845 records split among parent parishes. Jonesboro area families. Anglo-American settlement predominates with Protestant church records (Baptist, Methodist) as primary religious sources. Jackson Parish Library has local genealogical materials. Named for Andrew Jackson. Cross-reference with Claiborne and Union for earlier records. Timber industry heritage. Caney Creek and Dugdemona River area settlement patterns.

county_metadata.research_tips: Louisiana State Archives and Jefferson Parish Clerk of Court (Gretna). Formed 1825 from Orleans. Just across the Mississippi from New Orleans — the West Bank. Gretna Historical Society has local records. Jefferson Parish Library East Bank Regional Library has genealogy collections. Catholic church records at multiple parishes. Significant Italian immigrant community in Kenner and Gretna areas. German settlement along the river (Westwego area). Pre-1825 records in Orleans Parish. Notarial records at Jefferson Parish Clerk. Cross- reference extensively with Orleans Parish records. Hurricane Katrina (2005) caused some damage but records largely survived.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Louisiana parish formed 1912; 20th-century formation; Acadian/Cajun heartland
county_metadata.research_tips: Louisiana State Archives and Jefferson Davis Parish Clerk of Court (Jennings). Formed 1912 from Calcasieu — a young parish. Pre-1912 records in Calcasieu. Named for Confederate President Jefferson Davis. Southwest Louisiana rice farming country. Jennings was an oil boom town (first Louisiana oil well, 1901). Jeff Davis Parish Library has local history. Catholic and Protestant churches both important. Cross-reference with Calcasieu and Acadia for related families. Mixed Cajun and Midwestern American settler heritage — Iowa Colony brought Midwestern farmers to the rice prairies in the 1880s.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Louisiana parish formed 1908; 20th-century formation; Central Louisiana
county_metadata.research_tips: Louisiana State Archives and LaSalle Parish Clerk of Court (Jena). Formed 1908 from Catahoula — a relatively young parish. Pre-1908 records in Catahoula (burned parish — check Louisiana State Archives for surviving copies). Central Louisiana — piney woods and farming. Named for Rene-Robert Cavelier, Sieur de La Salle, French explorer. Jena Band of Choctaw Indians — unrecognized but culturally significant. LaSalle Parish Library has local history. Protestant church records predominate. Cross-reference with Catahoula, Grant, and Rapides for related families. Timber industry heritage.

county_metadata.parent_county: Attakapas County (became St. Martin, St. Mary)
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Louisiana parish formed 1823; antebellum parish; Acadian/Cajun heartland; significant free people of color community
county_metadata.research_tips: Louisiana State Archives and Lafayette Parish Clerk of Court (Lafayette). Formed 1823 from Attakapas County. Capital of Cajun Country — University of Louisiana at Lafayette (formerly USL) Center for Louisiana Studies has extensive Cajun and Creole manuscript collections. Lafayette Parish Clerk's notarial archives are rich. Catholic church registers at St. John Cathedral (Lafayette) date to the early 1800s. Acadian Cultural Center (NPS) documents Acadian dispersal and settlement. Vermilionville living history museum. Lafayette Parish Library has genealogy collections. Acadian, Creole, and African American heritage. Attakapas Native American presence in colonial records.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Louisiana parish formed 1807; original Orleans Territory parish; Acadian/Cajun heartland
county_metadata.research_tips: Louisiana State Archives and Lafourche Parish Clerk of Court (Thibodaux). Original Orleans Territory parish (1807). Bayou Lafourche — the longest bayou in Louisiana — was the highway for Acadian settlement. Thibodaux Massacre (1887) was a violent suppression of sugar workers. Sugar plantation parish with extensive enslaved persons records. Catholic church registers at St. Joseph Co-Cathedral (Thibodaux) date to colonial era. Nicholls State University Ellender Memorial Library has regional collections. Edward Douglass White Historic Site (Chief Justice). Cross-reference with Assumption and Terrebonne for Bayou Lafourche families.
